titleStmt.title: Superconductivity in Cu-doped Bi 2 Se 3 with potential disorder

analytic.title: Superconductivity in Cu-doped Bi 2 Se 3 with potential disorder

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: We study the effects of random nonmagnetic impurities on superconducting transition temperature Tc in a Cu-doped Bi2Se3, for which four types of pair potentials have been proposed. Although all the candidates belong to s-wave symmetry, two orbital degree of freedom in electronic structures enriches the symmetry variety of a Cooper pair such as even-orbital-parity and odd-orbital-parity. We consider realistic electronic structures of Cu-doped Bi2Se3 by using tight-binding Hamiltonian on a hexagonal lattice and consider effects of impurity scatterings through the self-energy of the Green's function within the Born approximation. We find that even-orbital-parity spin-singlet superconductivity is basically robust even in the presence of impurities. The degree of the robustness depends on the electronic structures in the normal state and on the pairing symmetry in orbital space. On the other hand, two odd-orbital-parity spin-triplet order parameters are always fragile in the presence of potential disorder.

div.head: I. INTRODUCTION
div.p: The robustness of superconductivity in the presence of nonmagnetic impurities depends on symmetry of the pair potential. The transition temperature T c is insensitive to the impurity concentration in a spin-singlet s-wave superconductor [1][2][3] . In a cuprate superconductor, on the other hand, T c of a spin-singlet d-wave superconductivity is suppressed drastically by the impurity scatterings 4 . The pair potential of an unconventional superconductor changes its sign on the Fermi surface depending on the direction of a quasiparticle's momenta. The random impurity scatterings make the motion of a quasiparticle be isotropic in both real and momentum spaces. Such a diffused quasiparticle feels the pair potential averaged over the directions of momenta. The resulting pair potential is finite for an s-wave symmetry, whereas it is zero for unconventional pairing symmetries. Thus, unconventional superconductivity is fragile under the potential disorder.

Previous papers [5][6][7][8][9] showed that s-wave superconductivity is not always robust against the nonmagnetic impurity scatterings in multiband (multiorbital) superconductors. The interorbital impurity scatterings decrease T c , which is a common conclusion of all the theoretical studies. The two-band models considered in these papers, however, are too simple to discuss the effects of impurities on T c in real materials such as iron pnictides 10,11 , MgB 2 12,13 , and Cu-doped Bi 2 Se 3 14,15 . The robustness of multiband superconductivity under the potential disorder may depend on electronic structures near the Fermi level. In iron pnictides and MgB 2 , two electrons in the same conduction band form a Cooper pair 11,13 . The impurity effect on such an intraband pair has been studied by taking realistic electronic structures into account 16 . In the case of Cu-doped Bi 2 Se 3 , four types of pair potentials Ōłå 1 -Ōłå 4 have been proposed as a promising candidate of order parameter 15 . Among them, an interorbital pairing order has attracted much attention as a topologically nontrivial superconductivity 15,17 . Unfortunately, the possibility of such a topological superconductivity under the potential disorder has never been studied yet. We address this issue.

In this paper, we study the effects of impurities on T c of Cu-doped Bi 2 Se 3 . We describe electronic structures near the Fermi level by taking into account two p orbitals in Bi 2 Se 3 and the hybridization between them 18,19 . According to the theoretical proposal 15 , we consider four types of s-wave pair potential on such orbital based electronic structures. The effects of impurities on T c are estimated through the impurity self-energy within the Born approximation. The transition temperature is calculated by solving the gap equation numerically and is plotted as a function of impurity concentration n imp . We will show that the relation between T c and n imp depends sensitively on the types of pair potentials. Superconductivity with an intraorbital pair potential Ōłå 1 is robust even in the dirty regime. This conclusion is consistent with that at a limiting case of previous studies [6][7][8] . There are two kinds of interorbital pairing order: even-orbital symmetry and odd-orbital symmetry. We find that T c of an even-interorbital superconductivity Ōłå 3 decreases slowly with the increase of n imp and vanishes in the dirty limit. The results for Ōłå 3 disagree with those in a simple two-band model 9 because the robustness of Ōłå 3 depends sensitively on electronic structures. Finally, the odd-interorbital pairing orders (Ōłå 2 and Ōłå 4 ) vanish at a critical value of the impurity concentration, which agrees well with the results of a idealistic two-band model 9 . Thus we conclude that odd-orbital pair potential is fragile irrespective of electronic structures. This paper is organized as follows. In Sec. II, we describe the effective Hamiltonian near the Fermi level in Cu-doped Bi 2 Se 3 and four types of pair potentials in its superconducting state. The anomalous Green's func-tion and the gap equation for each pair potential in the clean limit are obtained by solving the Gor'kov equation. In Sec. III, we introduce the random impurity potential and discuss the effects of impurities on T c within the Born approximation. The conclusion is given in Sec. IV. Throughout this paper, we use the units of k B = = 1, where k B is the Boltzmann constant. The symbol For constructing an effective model of the normal state, we start with the tight-binding Hamiltonian on a hexagonal lattice as shown in Fig. 1 20 . Strictly speaking, the crystal structure of Bi 2 Se 3 is rhombohedral 18,19 . The simplification does not affect the low energy physics. We assume that an intercalated copper atom supplies electrons and makes a topological insulator Bi 2 Se 3 be metallic 21 . In the hexagonal lattice, the primitive lat-
div.formula: FIG. 1.
div.p: The simplified lattice structure of a Cu-doped Bi2Se3. The arrow indicates the hopping.

tice vectors are ŌłÜ 3a/2, a/2, 0 , (0, a, 0), (0, 0, c) where a and c are the lattice constants in the xy plane and along the z axis, respectively. We define the nearest neighbor vectors a 1 = ŌłÜ 3a/2, a/2, 0 , a 2 = (0, a, 0), a 3 = -ŌłÜ 3a/2, a/2, 0 , and a 4 = (0, 0, c). The tightbinding Hamiltonian in real space can be written as 20,22
div.formula: H N = R Žł ŌĆĀ R ╬ĄŽł R + R,i Žł ŌĆĀ R ┼źai Žł R+ai + H.c.,(1)
Žł R = Žł P 1 + z ,Ōåæ (R) , Žł P 2 - z ,Ōåæ (R) , Žł P 1 + z ,Ōåō (R) , Žł P 2 - z ,Ōåō (R) T ,(2)
div.p: where Žł ŌĆĀ Žā,s (Žł Žā,s ) is the creation (annihilation) operator of an electron at the orbital Žā (= P 1 + z or P 2 - z ) with spin s (=Ōåæ or Ōåō). We consider only the nearest neighbor hopping on the hexagonal lattice in the xy plane and that along the z axis. An orbital P 1 + z (P 2 - z ) mainly consists of p z orbital of a Bi (Se) atom. The matrix element of hopping ┼źai (i = 1 -4) is described as
div.formula: R, Žā, s| H |R + a i , Žā ŌĆ▓ , s ŌĆ▓ .(3)
div.p: The nearest neighbor hopping elements are illustrated in Fig. 1. In momentum space, the tight-binding Hamiltonian is described as
div.formula: ╚×N (k) = ╬Ą + i ┼źai e ikŌĆóai + H.c..(4)
div.p: The matrix structures of ┼źai are given in Appendix A.
The tight-binding Hamiltonian can be written as
div.formula: ╚×N (k) = c k ┼Ø0 Žā0 + m k ┼Ø0 Žā3 + V z ┼Ø0 Žā2 + (V y ┼Ø1 -V x ┼Ø2 )Žā 1 ,(5)
div.p: where ╬▒ i (k) (i = 1, 2, x, y, z) is
div.formula: ╬▒ 2 (k) = 4 3a 2 3 -2 cos ŌłÜ 3k x a 2 cos k y a 2 -cos k y a ,(11)
╬▒ x (k) = 2 ŌłÜ 3a sin ŌłÜ 3k x a 2 cos k y a 2 , (12
╬▒ y (k) = 2 3a cos ŌłÜ 3k x a 2 sin k y a 2 + sin k y a ,(13)
╬▒ z (k) = 1 c sin k z c. (14
div.p: We define the Pauli matrices ┼Øj in spin space, Žāj in orbital space, and Žäj in particle-hole space for j = 1 -3. The unit matrix in these spaces are ┼Ø0 , Žā0 , and Žä0 . In Eq. ( 5), the hopping in the z direction ( ┼źa4 ) causes the orbital hybridization term V z and the hopping in the xy plane ( ┼źa1 , ┼źa2 , ┼źa3 ) causes the spin-orbit interaction term V x,y . When we expand the trigonometric functions around the ╬ō point, the tight-binding Hamiltonian ╚×N (k) corresponds to k ŌĆó p Hamiltonian of Bi 2 Se 3 18,19 .
The superconducting state in Cu x Bi 2 Se 3 is described by a Hamiltonian
div.formula: Žł e (k) = ’Ż« ’Ż» ’Ż» ’Ż░ Žł P 1 + z ,Ōåæ (k) Žł P 2 - z ,Ōåæ (k) Žł P 1 + z ,Ōåō (k) Žł P 2 - z ,Ōåō (k) ’Ż╣ ’Ż║ ’Ż║ ’Ż╗ , Žł h (k) = ’Ż« ’Ż» ’Ż» ’Ż» ’Ż» ’Ż░ Žł ŌĆĀ P 1 + z ,Ōåæ (-k) Žł ŌĆĀ P 2 - z ,Ōåæ (-k) Žł ŌĆĀ P 1 + z ,Ōåō (-k) Žł ŌĆĀ P 2 - z ,Ōåō (-k) ’Ż╣ ’Ż║ ’Ż║ ’Ż║ ’Ż║ ’Ż╗ ,(16)
div.p: According to the previous proposal 15 , we consider four types of momentum-independent pair potential defined by
div.formula: Ōłå 1 = g 1 N k Žł P 1 + z ,Ōåæ (k)Žł P 1 + z ,Ōåō (-k) = g 1 N k Žł P 2 - z ,Ōåæ (k)Žł P 2 - z ,Ōåō (-k) ,(18)
Ōłå 2 = g 2 N k Žł P 1 + z ,Ōåæ (k)Žł P 2 - z ,Ōåō (-k) = - g 2 N k Žł P 2 - z ,Ōåæ (k)Žł P 1 + z ,Ōåō (-k) ,(19)
Ōłå 3 = g 3 N k Žł P 1 + z ,Ōåæ (k)Žł P 2 - z ,Ōåō (-k) = g 3 N k Žł P 2 - z ,Ōåæ (k)Žł P 1 + z ,Ōåō (-k) ,(20)
Ōłå 4 = g 4 N k Žł P 1 + z ,Ōåæ (k)Žł P 2 - z ,Ōåæ (-k) = - g 4 N k Žł P 2 - z ,Ōåæ (k)Žł P 1 + z ,Ōåæ (-k) ,(21)
div.p: where g ╬╗ > 0 (╬╗ = 1 -4) represents the attractive interaction between two electrons. Generally speaking, the pair correlation function can be represented as
div.formula: f s,Žā;s ŌĆ▓ ,Žā ŌĆ▓ (k) = Žł s,Žā (k) Žł s ŌĆ▓ ,Žā ŌĆ▓ (-k) ,(22)
div.p: where we assume a spatially uniform equal-time Cooper pair. The momentum-symmetry is even-parity s-wave symmetry, which is a common property among the four candidates in a Cu-doped Bi 2 Se 3 . Because of the Fermi-Dirac statistics of electrons, the pairing correlation obeys
div.formula: f s,Žā;s ŌĆ▓ ,Žā ŌĆ▓ (k) = -f s ŌĆ▓ ,Žā ŌĆ▓ ;s,Žā (k). (23
div.p: The remaining symmetry options of the pairing function are orbitals and spins of a Cooper pair. Therefore, the pairing function must be either antisymmetric under s Ōåö s ŌĆ▓ or antisymmetric under Žā Ōåö Žā ŌĆ▓ . Both Eqs. ( 18) and ( 20) belong to spin-singlet symmetry. Thus the pairing functions belong to even-orbital parity. In Eq. ( 20), a Cooper pair consists of two electrons in the different orbitals (interorbital pair): one electron is in P 1 + z orbital and the other is in P 2 - z orbital. In Eq. ( 18), on the other hand, a Cooper pair consists of two electrons in the same orbital (intraorbital pair). The pair potential in the P 1 + z orbital and that in the P 2 - z orbital have the same amplitude and the same sign.

Both Eqs. ( 19) and ( 21) represent the spin-triplet interorbital pairing correlations. In these cases, the pair correlation belongs to odd-orbital-parity symmetry. In addition to the symmetry options for Cooper pairing, the pair potentials are classified by the irreducible representation of D 3d point group. Ōłå 2 and Ōłå 4 can be distinguished from each other by the irreducible representation. The matrix form of pair potentials, the irreducible representation, spin symmetry, and orbital-parity of the pair potentials are summarized in Table I. Although Fu and Berg 15 proposed a pair potential of Ōłå(i┼Ø 2 )Žā 3 , it is unitary equivalent to Eq. ( 18) as long as the Hamiltonian H(0) k preserves time-reversal symmetry 8 . (See Appendix B for details.) They also considered a pair potential of Ōłå┼Ø 0 (iŽā 2 ) independently of Eq. ( 21). However, the behavior of T c under the potential disorder in the two pair potentials are the same with each other. Thus, in this paper, we discuss effects of random impurity scatterings on superconducting states described by Eqs. ( 18)- (21). We note that the orbital parity and the momentum parity are independent symmetry options of each other. The former represents symmetry of correlation function under the commutation of two orbitals. The latter is derived from inversion symmetry of the lattice structure. The Matsubara Green's function is obtained by solving the Gor'kov equation,
div.formula: ßĖĀ(0) (k, iŽē n ) = Ū”(0) (k, iŽē n ) F (0) ╬╗ (k, iŽē n ) - F (0) * ╬╗ (-k, iŽē n ) -Ū”(0) * (-k, iŽē n ) ,(25)
div.p: where Žē n = (2n + 1)ŽĆT is a fermionic Matsubara frequency and T is a temperature. To discuss the transition temperature, we need to find the solutions of Eq. ( 24) within the first order of Ōłå. The results of the normal part
div.formula: Ū”(0) (k, iŽē n ) = 1 X [(iŽē n -c k ) ┼Ø0 Žā0 + m k ┼Ø0 Žā3 + V z ┼Ø0 Žā2 + (V y ┼Ø1 -V x ┼Ø2 ) Žā1 ] ,(26)
X(k, iŽē n ) = (iŽē n -c k ) 2 -m 2 k -V 2 x -V 2 y -V 2 z ,(27)
div.p: are common for all the pair potentials because the normal Green's function does not include the pair potential at the lowest order. The results of anomalous Green's function are given by,
div.formula: F (0) 1 (k, iŽē n ) = Ōłå 1 Z -i Žē 2 n + c 2 k + m 2 k + V 2 x + V 2 y + V 2 z ┼Ø2 Žā0 +2ic k m k ┼Ø2 Žā3 + 2ic k V z ┼Ø2 Žā2 -2c k V y ┼Ø3 Žā1 -2ic k V x ┼Ø0 Žā1 ] ,(28)

F (0) 2 (k, iŽē n ) = Ōłå 2 Z -i Žē 2 n + c 2 k -m 2 k + V 2 x + V 2 y + V 2 z ┼Ø1 Žā2 + 2m k V y ┼Ø0 Žā0 -2c k V y ┼Ø0 Žā3 + 2im k V x ┼Ø3 Žā0 -2ic k V x ┼Ø3 Žā3 +2ic k V z ┼Ø1 Žā0 -2im k V z ┼Ø1 Žā3 + 2iŽē n m k ┼Ø1 Žā1 ] ,(29)

F (0) 3 (k, iŽē n ) = Ōłå 3 Z -i Žē 2 n + c 2 k -m 2 k + V 2 x + V 2 y -V 2 z ) ┼Ø2 Žā1 + 2iV x V z ┼Ø0 Žā2 + 2V y V z ┼Ø3 Žā2 -2ic k V x ┼Ø0 Žā0 + 2im k V x ┼Ø0 Žā3 -2c k V y ┼Ø3 Žā0 + 2m k V y ┼Ø3 Žā3 -2iŽē n m k ┼Ø2 Žā2 + 2iŽē n V z ┼Ø2 Žā3 ] ,(30)

F (0) 4 (k, iŽē n ) = Ōłå 4 Z -i Žē 2 n + c 2 k -m 2 k + V 2 x -V 2 y + V 2 z ┼Ø3 Žā2 -2V x V y ┼Ø0 Žā2 -2im k V x ┼Ø1 Žā0 + 2ic k V x ┼Ø1 Žā3 + 2ic k V z ┼Ø3 Žā0 -2im k V z ┼Ø3 Žā3 -2V y V z ┼Ø2 Žā1 + 2iŽē n m k ┼Ø3 Žā1 -2Žē n V y ┼Ø2 Žā3 ] ,(31)
div.p: with
div.formula: Z(k, iŽē n ) = |X(k, iŽē n )| 2 .
div.p: The ┼Ø2 Žā0 component in Eq. ( 28), the ┼Ø1 Žā2 component in Eq. ( 29), the ┼Ø2 Žā1 component in Eq. ( 30), and the ┼Ø3 Žā2 component in Eq. ( 31) are linked to the pair potentials Ōłå 1 , Ōłå 2 , Ōłå 3 , and Ōłå 4 , respectively. Therefore, the gap equations in the linear regime result in
div.formula: Ōłå 1 = -g 1 T Žēn 1 N k Tr F (0) 1 (k, iŽē n ) (-i┼Ø 2 )Žā 0 4 = g 1 T Žēn 1 N k Ōłå 1 Z(k, iŽē n ) Žē 2 n + c 2 k + m 2 k + V 2 x + V 2 y + V 2 z ,(32)
Ōłå 2 = -g 2 T Žēn 1 N k Tr F (0) 2 (k, iŽē n ) ┼Ø1 (-iŽā 2 ) 4 = g 2 T Žēn 1 N k Ōłå 2 Z(k, iŽē n ) Žē 2 n + c 2 k -m 2 k + V 2 x + V 2 y + V 2 z ,(33)
Ōłå 3 = -g 3 T Žēn 1 N k Tr F (0) 3 (k, iŽē n ) (-i┼Ø 2 )Žā 1 4 = g 3 T Žēn 1 N k Ōłå 3 Z(k, iŽē n ) Žē 2 n + c 2 k -m 2 k + V 2 x + V 2 y -V 2 z ,(34)
Ōłå 4 = -g 4 T Žēn 1 N k Tr F (0) 4 (k, iŽē n ) ┼Ø3 (-iŽā 2 ) 4 = g 4 T Žēn 1 N k Ōłå 4 Z(k, iŽē n ) Žē 2 n + c 2 k -m 2 k + V 2 x -V 2 y + V 2 z .(35)
div.p: Eqs. ( 28), ( 29), (30), and (31) show that the orbital hybridization (V z ), the spin-orbit interaction (V x,y ), and the asymmetry between the two orbitals (m k ) generate various paring correlations which belong to different symmetry classes from that of the pair potential 23,24 . Especially, we discuss briefly a role of odd-frequency pairing correlation in the gap equation. For instance, the pairing correlation F (0) 2 includes 2iŽē n m k ┼Ø1 Žā1 which describes a spintriplet even-orbital-parity component. Such an component must be odd-frequency symmetry because the pairing correlation function must be antisymmetric under the permutation of two electrons. In the gap equation, the odd-frequency pairing component decreases the numerator as shown in -m 2 k in Eq. ( 33). It has been pointed out that an odd-frequency pair decreases the transition temperature 24 . If we would be able to tune the parameters to delete more the odd-frequency components, the gap equation results in higher T c .

div.head: III. EFFECTS OF DISORDER
div.p: We consider the random nonmagnetic impurities described by
div.formula: Himp (R) = V imp (R) Žä3 ┼Ø0 (Žā 0 + Žā1 ). (36
div.p: The schematic picture of potential disorder in a Cu x Bi 2 Se 3 is shown in Fig. 2. We assume the impurity potential satisfies the following properties,
div.formula: ) V imp (R)V imp (R ŌĆ▓ ) = n imp v 2 imp ╬┤ R,R ŌĆ▓ ,(38)
div.p: where ŌĆó ŌĆó ŌĆó means the ensemble average, n imp is the density of the impurities, and v imp is the strength of the impurity potential. We also assume that the attractive interactions between two electrons are insensitive to the impurity potentials 3 . We calculate the Green's function in the presence of the impurity potentials within the Born approximation. The Green's function is expanded up to the second order of the impurity potential.
div.formula: ßĖĀ(R -R ŌĆ▓ , Žē n ) Ōēł ßĖĀ(0) (R -R ŌĆ▓ , Žē n ) + R1 ßĖĀ(0) (R -R 1 , Žē n ) Himp (R 1 ) ßĖĀ(0) (R 1 -R ŌĆ▓ , Žē n ) + R1,R2 ßĖĀ(0) (R -R 1 , Žē n ) ├Ś Himp (R 1 ) ßĖĀ(0) (R 1 -R 2 , Žē n ) Himp (R 2 ) ├Ś ßĖĀ(R 2 -R ŌĆ▓ , Žē n ),(39)

Ōēł ßĖĀ(0) (R -R ŌĆ▓ , Žē n ) + n imp v 2 imp R1 ßĖĀ(0) (R -R 1 , Žē n ) Žä3 ┼Ø0 Žā0 ├Ś ßĖĀ(0) (0, Žē n ) Žä3 ┼Ø0 Žā0 ßĖĀ(R 1 -R ŌĆ▓ , Žē n ) + n imp v 2 imp R1 ßĖĀ(0) (R -R 1 , Žē n ) Žä3 ┼Ø0 Žā1 ├Ś ßĖĀ(0) (0, Žē n ) Žä3 ┼Ø0 Žā1 ßĖĀ(R 1 -R ŌĆ▓ , Žē n ). (40
div.p: We transform the Eq. ( 39) to (40) by using the properties in Eqs. (37) and (38). In momentum space, Eq. (40
div.formula: ) becomes ßĖĀ(k, iŽē n ) = ßĖĀ(0) (k, iŽē n ) + ßĖĀ(0) (k, iŽē n ) ╬Żimp ßĖĀ(k, iŽē n ), (41) ╬Żimp = ╬Żintra + ╬Żinter , (42) ╬Żintra = n imp v 2 imp Žä3 ┼Ø0 Žā0 1 N k ßĖĀ(0) (k, iŽē n )Žä 3 ┼Ø0 Žā0 , (43) ╬Żinter = n imp v 2 imp Žä3 ┼Ø0 Žā1 1 N k ßĖĀ(0) (k, iŽē n )Žä 3 ┼Ø0 Žā1 ,(44)
div.p: where ╬Żintra and ╬Żinter are the self-energy due to the intraorbital impurity scatterings and that of the interorbital impurity scatterings, respectively. We describe the total self-energy as follows.
div.formula: ╬Żimp = ╬Żintra + ╬Żinter = ╬Żg ╬Żf ╬╗ -╬Ż * f ╬╗ -╬Ż * g ,(45)
╬Żg = n imp v 2 imp [Ū¦ (0) + ┼Ø0 Žā1 Ū¦(0) ┼Ø0 Žā1 ],(46)
div.p: where we denote the momentum summation of the Green's function as
div.formula: 1/N k Ū”(0) (k, iŽē n ) = Ū¦(0) and 1/N k F (0) (k, iŽē n ) = f (0)
div.p: . Therefore, the Gor'kov equation in the presence of the impurity potential is described by
div.formula: iŽē n -H0 (k) -╬Żimp ßĖĀ(k, iŽē n ) = 1,(48)
ßĖĀ(k, iŽē n ) = Ū”(k, iŽē n ) F╬╗ (k, iŽē n ) -F * ╬╗ (-k, iŽē n ) -Ū” * (-k, iŽē n ) .(49)
div.p: The normal part of self-energy ( ╬Żg ) is calculated as follows.
div.formula: ╬Żg = [-iŽē n ╬Ę n + I n ] ┼Ø0 Žā0 ,(50)
╬Ę n =n imp v 2 imp 1 N k 2 Z ├Ś Žē 2 n + c 2 k + m 2 k + V 2 x + V 2 y + V 2 z ,(51)
I n =n imp v 2 imp 1 N k -2c k Z ├Ś Žē 2 n + c 2 k -m 2 k -V 2 x -V 2 y -V 2 z . (52
div.p: Within the first order of Ōłå, the normal Green's function becomes
div.formula: Ū”(k, iŽē n ) = Ū”(0) (k, iŽē n )| ┬ĄŌåÆ╬╝ , (53) Žēn = Žē n (1 + ╬Ę n ), (54
div.p: The imaginary (real) part of the self-energy renormalizes the Matsubara frequency (chemical potential). The anomalous Green's function after summing up the mo-menta is described as
div.formula: f (0) 1 = 1 N k Ōłå 1 Z ├Ś -i Žē 2 n + c 2 k + m 2 k + V 2 x + V 2 y + V 2 z ┼Ø2 Žā0 +2icm ┼Ø2 Žā3 ] ,(56)
f (0) 2 = 1 N k Ōłå 2 Z ├Ś -i Žē 2 n + c 2 k -m 2 k + V 2 x + V 2 y + V 2 z ┼Ø1 Žā2 +2iŽē n m k ┼Ø1 Žā1 ] ,(57)
f (0) 3 = 1 N k Ōłå 3 Z ├Ś -i Žē 2 n + c 2 k -m 2 k + V 2 x + V 2 y -V 2 z ┼Ø2 Žā1 -2iŽē n m ┼Ø2 Žā2 ] ,(58)
f (0) 4 = 1 N k Ōłå 4 Z ├Ś -i Žē 2 n + c 2 k -m 2 k + V 2 x -V 2 y + V 2 z ┼Ø3 Žā2 +2iŽē n m k ┼Ø3 Žā1 ] . (59
div.p: By substituting these expressions into Eq. ( 47), we obtain the anomalous part of the self-energy for each pair potential.
div.formula: ╬Żf1 = Ōłå 1 (i┼Ø 2 )Žā 0 ŌĆó ╬Ę n , (60) ╬Żf2 = Ōłå 2 ┼Ø1 Žā1 ŌĆó (-iŽē n )J n , (61) ╬Żf3 = Ōłå 3 (i┼Ø 2 )Žā 1 ŌĆó ╬Ę ŌĆ▓ n , (62) ╬Żf4 = Ōłå 4 ┼Ø3 Žā1 ŌĆó (-iŽē n )J n , (63
╬Ę ŌĆ▓ n = n imp v 2 imp 1 N k 2 Z ├Ś Žē 2 n + c 2 k -m 2 k + V 2 x + V 2 y -V 2 z ,(64)
J n = n imp v 2 imp 1 N k 4m k Z . (65
div.p: Before demonstrating T c under the potential disorder, we briefly summarize a relation between the self-energy and the pair potential in the four cases. The results in Eq. ( 60) show that ╬Żf1 has the same matrix structure with the pair potential as shown in Table . I. Namely, ╬Żf1 renormalizes the pair potential Ōłå 1 which belongs to even-frequency spin-singlet even-momentum-parity evenorbital-parity (ESEE) pairing symmetry. We will show that this fact explains the robustness of Ōłå 1 in the presence of impurity scatterings. The same feature can be seen in ╬Żf3 in Eq. ( 62), which implies the robustness of Ōłå 3 . On the other hand, ╬Żf2 and ╬Żf4 have the different matrix structure from their pair potentials shown in Table. I. In other words, the impurity self-energy leaves the pair potentials as they are. The previous studies suggested that the superconductivity in such cases can be fragile. We also note that ╬Żf2 and ╬Żf4 enhance the pair correlation belonging to odd-frequency spin-triplet even-momentum-parity even-orbital-parity (OTEE) symmetry. In what follows, we discuss characteristic behavior of T c as a function of impurity concentration case by case.
div.formula: Ōłå 1 Ōłå 2 Ōłå 3 Ōłå 4 T c /T 0 ╬Š 0 /Ōäō FIG.
div.p: 3. The superconducting transition temperature Tc is plotted as a function of ╬Š0/Ōäō. The vertical axis is normalized to the transition temperature in the clean limit T0. We fix T0 for all pair potentials.

div.head: Ōłå1
div.p: The gap equation for Ōłå 1 results in
div.formula: Ōłå 1 = g 1 T Žēn 1 N k Ōłå1 Z ├Ś Žē2 n + c2 k + m 2 k + V 2 x + V 2 y + V 2 z . (66
div.p: By comparing with the gap equation in the clean limit in Eq. (32), the renormalized values are defined as
div.formula: Z(k, iŽē n ) = Z(k, iŽē n )| ┬ĄŌåÆ╬╝ , (68
) ck = c k | ┬ĄŌåÆ╬╝ .(69)
div.p: The impurity self-energy renormalizes the pair potential and the Matsubara frequency in the same manner as Ōłå 1 ŌåÆ Ōłå1 and Žē n ŌåÆ Žēn 1 . We solve the gap equation numerically and plot the transition temperature T c of Ōłå 1 as a function of ╬Š 0 /Ōäō in Fig. 3. Here T 0 is the transition temperature in the clean limit, ╬Š 0 = v F /(2ŽĆT 0 ) is the superconducting coherence length, v F is the Fermi velocity, Ōäō = v F Žä imp is the mean free path due to impurity scatterings, and Žä imp is the lifetime of a quasiparticle. We found that the normal part of self-energy ╬Żg is nearly independent of the Matsubara frequency in the low energy region for Žē n Ōēż Žē c . Here Žē c = 10 3 T 0 is the cut-off energy of the Matsubara frequency. Therefore, we estimate Žä imp from the imaginary part of ╬Żg as
div.formula: 1 Žä imp = -2Tr 1 4 Im ╬Żg Ōł╝ 2ŽĆ ├Ś n imp v 2 imp ├Ś 10 -2 [eV].(70)
div.p: The horizontal axis ╬Š 0 /Ōäō in Fig. 3 is proportional to the impurity concentration n imp . The results in Fig. 3 show that T c of Ōłå 1 is almost independent of the impurity concentration as shown with filled circles. Such behavior agrees well with T c in a limiting case of idealistic models. The previous papers [6][7][8] considered two-band superconductivity with the intraband pairing order parameters (say D 1 and D 2 ) on idealistic two-band electronic structures and demonstrated that T c is independent of impurity concentration at D 1 = D 2 . The interband impurity scatterings disappear in such a symmetric situation, which explains the unchanged T c . The superconducting state in Cu-doped Bi 2 Se 3 with Ōłå 1 corresponds to the symmetric intraband pairing state in the previous studies. In this paper, we confirmed that the conclusions of the previous papers on idealistic band structures are valid even if we calculate T c on a realistic electronic structure. In Fig. 3, the results for Ōłå 1 show the oscillating behavior. Although it is not easy to specify the reasons of the oscillations, such behavior comes from a realistic band structure. In the Born approximation, we conclude that T c of Ōłå 1 is insensitive to the impurity scatterings.

div.head: Ōłå3
div.p: The gap equation for Ōłå 3 becomes
div.formula: Ōłå 3 = g 3 T Žēn 1 N k Ōłå ŌĆ▓ 3 Z ├Ś Žē2 n + c2 k -m 2 k + V 2 x + V 2 y -V 2 z , (71) Ōłå ŌĆ▓ 3 = Ōłå 3 (1 + ╬Ę ŌĆ▓ n ). (72
div.p: The pair potential is renormalized by the impurity selfenergy as Ōłå 3 ŌåÆ Ōłå ŌĆ▓ 3 in Eq. ( 71) in a slightly different way from the relation Žē n ŌåÆ Žēn . By solving Eq. ( 71), we plot T c of Ōłå 3 as a function of ╬Š 0 /Ōäō in Fig. 3. The results show that T c of the spin-singlet interorbital pairing order is suppressed slowly with the increase of ╬Š 0 /Ōäō and goes to zero in the dirty limit. A previous paper 9 , however, demonstrated on an idealistic two-band structure that T c of a spin-singlet s-wave interband pairing order is indeof the impurity concentration. Thus Ōłå 3 in a Cu-doped Bi 2 Se 3 is more fragile than that in an idealistic two-band model. The difference between the results in the two models can be explained by the enhancement of odd-frequency pairing components due to the realistic electronic structures. The odd-frequency pairing correlation is absent in an idealistic band structure 9 . As a result, the impurity self-energy renormalizes the pair potential and the Matsubara frequency in the same manner, which leads to unchanged T c versus ╬Š 0 /Ōäō. In Cu-doped Bi 2 Se 3 , on the other hand, the asymmetry between twoorbitals (m k ) and the orbital hybridization (V z ) generate the odd-frequency pairing correlations as described in Eq. (30). These correlations contribute negatively to the numerator of the renormalization factor of the pair potential 1 + ╬Ę ŌĆ▓ n as shown in -m 2 k and -V 2 z in Eq. ( 64). As a consequence, the reduction of the pair potential by odd-frequency pairs causes the suppression of T c in the dirty regime. We conclude that the robustness of the spin-singlet s-wave interorbital pairing order depends on band structures.

div.head: Ōłå2 and Ōłå4
div.p: The gap equations for Ōłå 2 and Ōłå 4 result in
div.formula: Ōłå 2 = g 2 T Žēn 1 N k Ōłå 2 Z ├Ś Žē2 n + c2 k -m 2 k + V 2 x + V 2 y + V 2 z -2J n Žē n Žēn m k ] ,(73)
Ōłå 4 = g 4 T Žēn 1 N k Ōłå 4 Z ├Ś Žē2 n + c2 k -m 2 k + V 2 x -V 2 y + V 2 z -2J n Žē n Žēn m k ] .(74)
div.p: Both Ōłå 2 and Ōłå 4 represent spin-triplet interorbital pairing order antisymmetric under the permutation of two orbitals. The numerical results in Fig. 3 indicate that T c of Ōłå 2 and that of Ōłå 4 decrease rapidly with the increase of ╬Š 0 /Ōäō and vanish around ╬Š 0 /Ōäō Ōēł 0.3. The impurity self-energy renormalizes the Matsubara frequency as Žē n ŌåÆ Žēn . However, it leaves the pair potentials unchanged as shown in Eqs. ( 73) and (74). Therefore, Ōłå 2 and Ōłå 4 are fragile in the presence of impurities. The obtained results of T c for a Cu-doped Bi 2 Se 3 agree even quantitatively with those calculated in an idealistic band structure 9 . The interorbital impurity scatterings mix the electronic states in the two orbitals and average the pair potentials over the two orbital degree of freedom. As a result, the impurity scatterings wash out the sign of the pair potential in Eq. ( 17), which leads to the suppression of odd-orbital symmetric superconductivity. We confirmed that this physical interpretation is valid independent of band structures. Finally, we compare our results in the present paper with those in a recent study 25 . The authors of Ref. 25 formulated the random impurity scatterings based on the two-band picture in momentum space, which is obtained by diagonalizing the normal state Hamiltonian in the absence of impurities 26 . They mapped a Hamiltonian for an interorbital s-wave superconductor with random impurities to a Hamiltonian for a single-band unconventional superconductor with random impurities. As a result, they concluded that Ōłå 2 , Ōłå 3 , and Ōłå 4 are fragile under the potential disorder. Their conclusion for Ōłå 3 does not agree with ours obtained by applying the standard method 1 . The difference in the theoretical methods causes the discrepancy. A key point might be the self-energy due to interorbital impurity scatterings. Actually all of the previous papers [6][7][8][9] have suggested an importance of the interorbital/interband impurity scatterings on T c . Ref. 25, on the other hand, does not consider the interorbital impurity scatterings.

div.head: IV. CONCLUSION
div.p: We studied the effects of random nonmagnetic impurities on the superconducting transition temperature T c in Cu-doped Bi 2 Se 3 . We consider four types of momentumindependent pair potentials, which include the intraorbital pairing (Ōłå 1 ), the interorbital-even-parity pairing (Ōłå 3 ), and the interorbital-odd-parity pairings (Ōłå 2 and Ōłå 4 ). The effects of the impurity scatterings are considered through the self-energy of the Green's function within the Born approximation. T c of Ōłå 1 is insensitive to the impurity concentration, which is consistent with the previous theories. We find that Ōłå 1 with the electronic structure of a Cu-doped Bi 2 Se 3 corresponds to a limiting case of idealistic models [6][7][8] . T c of Ōłå 3 decreases moderately with the increase of impurity concentration and vanishes in the dirty limit, which does not agree well with the results on an idealistic model 9 . The presence of the odd-frequency pairing correlations explain the discrepancy. T c of Ōłå 2 and Ōłå 4 decrease rapidly with the increase of the impurity concentration. Superconductivity vanishes at a critical value of the impurity concentration. The results are consistent with those in an idealistic model even quantitatively 9 .

We found that the robustness of the even-orbital-parity order parameters depends on the details of the band structures and that the odd-orbital-parity order parameters are fragile irrespective of the band structures. In momentum space, the tight-binding Hamiltonian becomes
div.formula: ╚×N (k) = ’Ż½ ’Ż¼ ’ŻŁ c k + m k -i(v 3 ╬▒ 3 (k) + v z ╬▒ z (k)) 0 v(╬▒ y (k) + i╬▒ x (k)) i(v 3 ╬▒ 3 (k) + v z ╬▒ z (k)) c k -m k v(╬▒ y (k) + i╬▒ x (k)) 0 0 v(╬▒ y (k) -i╬▒ x (k)) c k + m k -i(v 3 ╬▒ 3 (k) + v z ╬▒ z (k)) v(╬▒ y (k) -i╬▒ x (k)) 0 i(v 3 ╬▒ 3 (k) + v z ╬▒ z (k)) c k -m k ’ŻČ ’ŻĘ ’ŻĖ , (A11)
div.p: with n being an integer for the Hamiltonian to preserve time-reversal symmetry. By tuning ╬Ė = 0 at n = 0, the two pair potentials have the same sign with each other because of ŽĢ 1 -ŽĢ 2 = 0. By tuning ╬Ė = ŽĆ/2, on the other hand, H(0) k (ŽĆ/2, 0, ŽĆ) describes a state where two pair potentials have the opposite sign to each other. It is easy to show that H(0) k (ŽĆ/2, 0, ŽĆ) and H(0) k (0, 0, 0) are unitary equivalent to each other. We set g P 1 + z = g P 2 - z = g 1 and Ōłå P 1 + z = Ōłå P 2 - z = Ōłå 1 in section II. Under the condition, Ōłå 1 (i┼Ø 2 )Žā 3 is unitary equivalent to Ōłå1 = Ōłå 1 (i┼Ø 2 )Žā 0 .

figure.head: FIG. 2 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 2.A model of the random potential in a CuxBi2Se3. The cross mark denotes an impurity.

figure.head: TABLE I .
figure.label: I
figure.figDesc: Symmetry classification of pair potentials. Equaltime pairing order parameter belongs to even-frequency symmetry. A spin-singlet component is described by i┼Ø2. An opposite-spin-triplet and an equal-spin-triplet components are indicated by ┼Ø1 and ┼Ø3, respectively.

t ŌĆ▓ 11 t ŌĆ▓ 12 -t ŌĆ▓ 12 t ŌĆ▓ 22 0 0 t ŌĆ▓ 0 0 11 t ŌĆ▓ 0 0 12 0 0 -t ŌĆ▓ 12 t ŌĆ▓ 22
t ŌĆ▓ 11 -t ŌĆ▓ 12 0 t ŌĆ▓ 12 t ŌĆ▓ 22 0 0 0 t ŌĆ▓ 11 -t ŌĆ▓ 0 0 0 0 t ŌĆ▓ 22 12 t ŌĆ▓ 12

note.p: Ōłå2┼Ø1(iŽā2) A1u Even Triplet Even Odd (Inter) Ōłå3(i┼Ø2)Žā1 A2u Even Singlet Even Even (Inter) Ōłå4┼Ø3(iŽā2) Eu Even Triplet Even Odd (Inter)

div.head: ACKNOWLEDGMENTS
div.p: The authors are grateful to P. M. R. Brydon, D. C. Cavanagh, and K. Yada for useful discussions. This work was supported by KAKENHI (No. 20H01857), JSPS Core-to-Core Program (A. Advanced Research Networks), and JSPS and Russian Foundation for Basic Research under Japan-Russia Research Cooperative Program Grant No. 19-52-50026.
Appendix A: Restriction of hopping matrix in tight-binding Hamiltonian

The crystal structure of Bi 2 Se 3 preserves discrete symmetries 18,19 such as threefold rotation R 3 along the z direction, twofold rotation R 2 along the x direction, and inversion P . In addition, both the normal and superconducting states preserve time-reversal T symmetry. With the basis of (|P 1
), these symmetry operations can be represented as R 3 = exp(i ŽĆ 3 s 3 Žā 0 ), R 2 = is 1 Žā 3 , P = s 0 Žā 3 , and T = is 2 Žā 0 K, respectively. Here K represents the complex conjugation.
Under threefold rotation symmetry, the relation
is satisfied for (a i , a j ) = (a 1 , -a 2 ), (a 2 , -a 3 ), and (a 3 , a 1 ). Under twofold rotation symmetry, the relation
holds true for (a i , a j ) = (a 1 , -a 3 ), (a 2 , -a 2 ), and (a 3 , -a 1 ). As a results of inversion symmetry, we find the relation of
Finally, time-reversal symmetry is described as
We have used the notation of
According to the conditions in Eqs. (A1), (A2), (A3), and (A4), the hopping matrices can be reduced as
Here ╬▒ 1 (k), ╬▒ 2 (k), ╬▒ x (k), ╬▒ y (k), and ╬▒ z (k), are defined in Eqs. ( 10)- (14). We also define

In this paper, we set the parameters as follows 20,27 : a = 4.14 ├ģ, c = 28.7 ├ģ, ┬Ą = 0.5 eV, c 2 = 30.4 eV ├ģ2 , m 0 = -0.28 eV, m 2 = 44.5 eV ├ģ2 , v = 3.33 eV ├ģ, c 1 /c 2 = 0.024 eV, m 1 /c 2 = 0.20 eV, and v z /c = 0.32 eV. We choose v 3 = 0 for simplicity 18,19 .

div.head: Appendix B: Unitary equivalence of the Hamiltonian with intraorbital pairing order
div.p: The superconducting state with s-wave spin-singlet intraorbital pairing order is described by a following Bogoliubovde Gennes Hamiltonian 8 .

where g Žā > 0 represents the attractive interaction between two electrons in the orbital Žā and ╬Ė denotes the phase of the hybridization in the normal state. We obtain the normal part of H(0) k (╬Ė, ŽĢ 1 , ŽĢ 2 ) from Eq. ( 5) by choosing Žł P 1 + z ,s ŌåÆ Žł P 1 + z ,s e i╬Ė/2 and Žł P 2 - z ,s ŌåÆ Žł P 2 - z ,s e -i╬Ė/2 . Although the phase factor e i╬Ė does not affect the physics in the normal state, such a gauge transformation affects the relative phase difference between the order parameters ŽĢ 1 -ŽĢ 2 8 . Time-reversal symmetry of H(0) k is represented by
If we find a transformation ┼¬ which eliminates all the phase factors in Eq. (B1), it is possible to show time-reversal symmetry of H(0)

monogr.title: Methods of Quantum Field Theory in Statistical Physics
